text: The surgeon opens the peritoneum of the mesentery inferior to the ileocolic vessels using monopolar forceps. A blunt dissection develops the avascular plane between the mesocolon and retroperitoneum. Lymphatic tissue at the ileomesenteric junctions and along the right side of the superior mesenteric vein is resected. The fourth robotic arm provides retraction with a fenestrated grasper, while the assistant applies countertraction using a laparoscopic instrument. Fenestrated bipolar forceps and monopolar curved scissors are used for the dissection.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum of the mesentery just below the ileocolic vessels using monopolar forceps. A blunt dissection develops the avascular plane between the mesocolon and retroperitoneum. Lymphatic tissue at the ileomesenteric junctions and along the right side of the superior mesenteric vein is completely resected. The fourth robotic arm provides retraction with a fenestrated grasper, while the assistant applies countertraction using a laparoscopic instrument. Fenestrated bipolar forceps and monopolar curved scissors are utilized for the dissection.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic artery and divides it using clips and a bipolar vessel sealing device. Subsequently, the ileocolic vein is isolated and dissected using the same technique.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic artery and divides it using clips and a bipolar vessel sealing device. Subsequently, the ileocolic vein is isolated and dissected using the same technique.

The surgeon continues the mesocolic plane dissection cranially towards the C-loop of the duodenum, exposing the pancreatic head. The right colic pedicle is isolated and dissected, with complete dissection performed over the preduodenal pancreatic fascia.

The surgeon continues the mesocolic plane dissection cranially, exposing the pancreatic head near the C-loop of the duodenum. The right colic pedicle is isolated and dissected, with complete dissection performed along the avascular plane over the preduodenal pancreatic fascia.

The surgeon divides the gastrocolic ligament, mobilizing the hepatic flexure. The transverse mesocolon and the right branch of the middle colic artery are transected, preparing the colon for transection.

The surgeon divides the gastrocolic ligament, reaching and mobilizing the hepatic flexure. The transverse mesocolon and the right branch of the middle colic artery are transected, preparing the colon for transection.

The surgeon completes dissection over the girotifacia, preserving the right testicular vessels and ureter. The tumor is adhered to the peritoneum in the right iliac region, necessitating an en bloc dissection of the cecum with the parietal peritoneum, including the right side of the bladder dome serosa. The bladder wall's muscular layer is not involved in the neoplastic invasion. An intraoperative methylene blue dye test confirms the bladder's integrity post-dissection.

The surgeon completes dissection over the girotifacia, preserving the right testicular vessels and ureter. The tumor adheres to the peritoneum in the right iliac region, necessitating an en bloc dissection of the cecum with the parietal peritoneum, including the right side of the bladder dome serosa. The bladder wall's muscular layer remains uninvolved by neoplastic invasion, confirmed by an intraoperative methylene blue dye test showing bladder integrity.

The surgeon releases adhesions tethering the ileum to the retroperitoneum, enhancing bowel mobility. The transverse colon and terminal ileum are transected using a robotic linear cutting stapler. Endocyanine green fluorescence angiography is performed, confirming the perfusion and viability of the colon.

The surgeon releases adhesions tethering the ileum to the retroperitoneum, enhancing bowel mobility. The transverse colon and terminal ileum are transected using a robotic linear cutting stapler. Endocyanine green fluorescence angiography is performed, confirming the perfusion and viability of the colon.

The detached specimen is placed over the right liver to increase working space. The transverse colon and ileum are aligned side-to-side, and enterotomies are made along the anti-mesenteric border. A linear robotic cutting stapler is inserted through the enterotomies to perform the intracorporeal ileocolic anastomosis. The enterotomy is closed with a robotic hand-sewn running suture, followed by a second seromuscular layer with single stitches. The specimen is extracted through an extended suprapubic trocar wound.

The specimen is placed over the right liver to maximize working space. The transverse colon and ileum are aligned side-to-side, and enterotomies are made along the anti-mesenteric border. The surgeon inserts a linear robotic guided cutting stapler through the enterotomies to perform the intracorporeal ileocolic anastomosis. A robotic hand-sewn running suture closes the enterotomy, followed by a second seromuscular layer with single stitches. The specimen is extracted through an extended sovrubic trochar wound.
